pattern: Building a minimum spanning forest and then cutting its k-1 heaviest tree edges minimizes the largest surviving edge weight across at most k components.
hints: Removing edges only ever splits components apart, so to reach at most k components you keep a spanning forest and the cost is the heaviest edge you keep.
Among all spanning forests, the one built greedily from the lightest edges keeps the smallest possible heavy edge for any target number of components.
Run Kruskal to collect the n-1 spanning-tree weights in ascending order, drop the k-1 largest by leaving them cut, and answer with the largest remaining kept weight (0 if none remain).

edge_inputs.description: cut the single heaviest edge to make 2 components
k=1 forbids any cut, cost is the global heaviest edge
k large enough to isolate every node, cost 0
extra heavy edge ignored by the MST
star graph, cutting leaves trims the max

stress.description: random connected weighted graph with spanning-tree backbone plus extra edges
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    edges = []
    order = list(range(n))
    rng.shuffle(order)
    for i in range(1, n):
        u = order[i]
        v = order[rng.randrange(0, i)]
        edges.append([u, v, rng.randrange(1, 1000001)])
    extra = rng.randrange(0, n)
    for _ in range(extra):
        u = rng.randrange(0, n)
        v = rng.randrange(0, n)
        if u != v:
            edges.append([u, v, rng.randrange(1, 1000001)])
    k = rng.randrange(1, n + 1)
    return [n, edges, k]
